---
title: "vellumverse #6: A vellum scene is just a table of elements"
author:
- name: David Schoch
  orcid: 0000-0003-2952-4812
date: '2026-07-27'
categories:
- R
- data visualization
- vellumverse
draft: true
---

Last post ended on a question: if a scene is a tree you can read back, what do
you actually get when you read it? The answer is almost boringly plain, and that
plainness is the point. You get a data frame, one row per drawn element. That
table is the contract the whole ecosystem binds to, so this post is about the
table.

The function is `scene_model()`. Build a small keyed scene and read it back.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(vellum)

s <- vl_scene(5, 3, bg = "white") |>
  draw(points_grob(
    x = c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75), y = c(0.4, 0.7, 0.4),
    size = vl_unit(6, "mm"), gp = vl_gpar(fill = "#3498db", col = NA),
    key  = c("a", "b", "c"),
    meta = list(list(tooltip = "first"),
                list(tooltip = "second"),
                list(tooltip = "third"))
  ))

m <- scene_model(s)
m$elements[, c("mark", "key", "x", "y", "w", "h")]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   mark key   x     y        w        h
1 point   a 120 172.8 45.35433 45.35433
2 point   b 240  86.4 45.35433 45.35433
3 point   c 360 172.8 45.35433 45.35433
```


:::
:::


Two things rode along on that `points_grob()` without changing the picture at
all. `key` is a data key per element, the join back to a row of your data. `meta`
is a free-form record per element, here a tooltip string. `scene_model()` walks
the rendered scene and returns one row per element of the keyable marks, pairing
each element's identity (its key, its mark kind, its panel) with its resolved
bounding box in device pixels. A second data frame, `m$panels`, does the same for
named panels.

The bounding box is the part you cannot get any other way. It comes from the
compiled backend after layout, so it is where the element *actually landed*, not
where you asked for it to go. That is what lets something outside the scene, a
browser for instance, place a tooltip in exactly the right spot.

Notice what did not happen. Adding `key` and `meta` changed not one pixel. The
contract is strict about this: keys and metadata are pure identity, and a scene
carrying them renders byte-for-byte identically to the same scene without them. A
static PNG never even calls `scene_model()`. The interactivity is all opt-in
metadata riding on top of an unchanged picture.

Here is the shape of the whole ecosystem in three lines, and every arrow is this
one table:

```text
vellumplot    compiles a plot into grobs carrying key + meta
vellum        renders the SVG, and emits scene_model(): identity + bbox
vellumwidget  reads the SVG + scene_model() to wire hover, select, brush
```

`vellumplot`, the grammar layer coming in the next post, does not reach into
`vellum`'s internals. It emits grobs with keys on them. `vellumwidget`, the interactive
layer, knows nothing about the grammar. It reads `scene_model()` and the matching
SVG, zips them together by paint order, and attaches behaviour. The `mark`
vocabulary is a closed set, the element order is guaranteed to match the SVG, and
`vellum`'s own tests assert that the identity columns and the geometry columns
enumerate the same elements in the same order. A mismatch is a hard error, never
a silent mis-join.

That is why I keep calling it a contract rather than an API. Three version-locked
packages depend on this table's columns, its ordering, and its additivity, and
pinning it down is what lets the grammar on top and the widget above that each be
written against a stable seam without knowing how the other works.

The series has stayed underground so far, down in the backend. `vellum` gives you
primitives, viewports, units, a retained editable tree, and this read-back table.
What it deliberately withholds is scales, geoms, and facets; those live one layer
up. Next post I reach that layer and start building actual statistical plots with
`vellumplot`, whose entire job is to compile a grammar down into the scene you
have spent the last few posts getting to know.
